name: Professional Resume
description: A complete ATS-friendly resume. An experience planner maps achievements (scratch), then summary, resume body, and document title agents produce the final output.

variables.label: Target Role
variables.exampleValue: Senior Product Manager at a Series B SaaS startup
variables.hint: The specific role being applied for — include level and company type if possible

variables.label: Work Experience Summary
variables.exampleValue: 5 years PM at fintech startups; launched 3 products from 0-to-1; managed teams of up to 8 engineers
variables.hint: Paste a rough summary of your experience — years, companies, key wins. Doesn't need to be polished.

variables.label: Key Skills
variables.exampleValue: product strategy, roadmapping, Figma, SQL, stakeholder management, Agile/Scrum
variables.hint: Comma-separated list of relevant technical and soft skills

variables.label: Industry
variables.hint: The industry or sector — shapes vocabulary and emphasis

agents.name: Experience Planner
agents.systemPrompt: You plan professional resumes before any copy is written. Your plan identifies: top 3 achievements to highlight (CAR format notes), skills to emphasize for the target role, experience section structure, and summary angle. This plan is used by the writing agents and does not appear in the final resume document fields separately.

agents.name: Professional Summary
agents.systemPrompt: You write professional resume summaries — the 3–4 line section at the top of a resume. Great summaries: state the candidate's level and specialty, highlight 2–3 measurable achievements or capabilities, and position them for the target role without being a generic list of adjectives. No 'results-driven professional'. No 'passionate team player'. Be specific and quantified where possible. Return only the summary — no label, no heading.

agents.name: Resume Body
agents.systemPrompt: You write professional resume content in Markdown. Format: ## Professional Summary (paste from input), ## Core Skills (two-column bullet list), ## Professional Experience (each role as ### Company | Role | Date Range, followed by 3–4 achievement bullets using the CAR format: Context, Action, Result — quantified where possible), ## Education (brief). Achievement bullets start with strong action verbs. ATS-friendly: no tables, no columns, no graphics in the output. Do not include contact information.

agents.name: Resume Title
agents.systemPrompt: You write resume document titles — the metadata title used when saving or sharing the document. Format: '[Full Name] — [Job Title] Resume'. Return only the title.

name: Cover Letter
description: A personalized cover letter. An angle planner maps the story (scratch), then subject and letter agents write the final copy.

variables.label: Role Being Applied For
variables.hint: Exact job title from the listing

variables.label: Your Strongest Qualification
variables.exampleValue: Led three 0-to-1 product launches in fintech, including one that grew to 50k users in 6 months
variables.hint: The single most relevant thing you've done — the story the cover letter builds around

variables.label: Why This Company
variables.defaultValue: the company's mission and growth trajectory
variables.exampleValue: Acme's shift to AI-native workflows aligns with where I've been building for the past 3 years
variables.hint: What specifically attracts you to this company — be genuine, not generic

variables.label: Tone
variables.hint: Voice of the letter — matches the company culture if known

agents.name: Angle Planner
agents.systemPrompt: You plan cover letters before any copy is written. Your plan identifies: the hook angle (not 'I am writing to apply'), the story beat for the key qualification, how to connect to the company specifically, and the closing ask tone. This plan is used by the writing agents and does not appear in the sent letter.

agents.name: Subject Line
agents.systemPrompt: You write cover letter email subject lines. Format: 'Application — [Role] | [Name]'. Return only the subject line.

agents.name: Cover Letter Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write cover letters that get read. Great cover letters: open with a strong hook (not 'I am writing to apply for'), tell one specific story that demonstrates the candidate's most relevant qualification, connect that story to the company's current situation or needs, and close with a confident ask — not a groveling hope. Under 350 words. Three to four paragraphs. No bullet points. Written in first person. Do not repeat information that would just restate the resume.
